content.assessment: This proposal most directly confronts the counterfactual-contraction question by accepting that default closure is appropriate where cohorts are genuinely below viability thresholds, while adding mitigation for the harms closure produces. It is the only proposal to explicitly engage the contested cross-national evidence on whether closure itself causes further population decline; the lens notes this evidence is genuinely mixed (Danish causal finding of 7.6 percentage point decline over ten years, contested elsewhere) and therefore the mitigation package's adequacy to offset second-round demographic effects remains uncertain rather than established.
opportunities: Threshold criteria could be calibrated directly to cohort-decline metrics (TFR, under-18 share, average age) to make closure decisions demographically transparent rather than discretionary
strengths: Directly addresses viability thresholds using transparent criteria, appropriate for settlements that have already crossed the zero-enrollment cliff (e.g., under-10-percent under-18 share)
Explicitly engages contested demographic evidence on closure-driven depopulation rather than assuming a single causal direction
threats: If Hungary's context resembles the Danish/Finnish pattern rather than the Saxony/Swedish no-link pattern, default closure under this model could accelerate exactly the depopulation dynamic it aims to manage, contradicting its own mitigation goals

Regional disparities (Zala's steeper decline) mean uniform national thresholds may trigger disproportionate closures in already-vulnerable counties, compounding existing spatial age-structure divergence
weaknesses: Mitigation measures (transport, funding transparency, equity support) do not address the delayed structural depopulation mechanism (social capital and housing-market lock-in) documented in the Danish study, which emerges only from year six onward and thus outlasts most transition-support programs
Contested cost-savings evidence (Texas vs. NY) means the fiscal rationale underlying threshold-triggered defaults is itself demographically and financially uncertain
